question: A rolling bearing rotating under load may ultimately suffer from material fatigue. Typically, fatigue damage is characterized by a small piece of material breaking away from the raceway leaving a cavity. This cavity may then propagate into a crack, and the bearing will fail. If a large batch of identical bearings is run under the same conditions until 10% of the batch has failed from the material fatigue damage, then the batch is said to have attained its L10 life. In other words, the remaining 90% of the bearings in the batch will survive for periods longer than the L10 life. Consider a rolling bearing which has a hazard-rate function in the form h(t) = [(1/θ)(t/θ)^(n−1)] / [(n−1)! Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} (t/θ)^k / k!], where n = 3 and θ = 290 hours. Determine the reliability of the bearing at t = 100 hours.

A rolling bearing rotating under load may ultimately suffer from material fatigue. Typically, fatigue damage is characterized by a small piece of material breaking away from the raceway leaving a cavity. This cavity may then propagate into a crack, and the bearing will fail. If a large batch of identical bearings is run under the same conditions until 10% of the batch has failed from the material fatigue damage, then the batch is said to have attained its L10 life. In other words, the remaining 90% of the bearings in the batch will survive for periods longer than the L10 life. Consider a rolling bearing which has a hazard-rate function in the form h(t) = [(1/θ)(t/θ)^(n−1)] / [(n−1)! Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} (t/θ)^k / k!], where n = 3 and θ = 290 hours. Determine the expected life E(t) = γθ of the bearing in hours.

A rolling bearing rotating under load may ultimately suffer from material fatigue. Typically, fatigue damage is characterized by a small piece of material breaking away from the raceway leaving a cavity. This cavity may then propagate into a crack, and the bearing will fail. If a large batch of identical bearings is run under the same conditions until 10% of the batch has failed from the material fatigue damage, then the batch is said to have attained its L10 life. In other words, the remaining 90% of the bearings in the batch will survive for periods longer than the L10 life. Consider a rolling bearing which has a hazard-rate function in the form h(t) = [(1/θ)(t/θ)^(n−1)] / [(n−1)! Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} (t/θ)^k / k!], where n = 3 and θ = 290 hours. Assuming L10 = 100 hours, determine the MRL of the bearing in hours.
Find the MTTF, assuming F(t) = 1 − (8/7)e^(−t) + (1/7)e^(−8t).

The failure time of a new brake drum design is observed to follow a Gamma distribution with a p.d.f. of f(t) = λ(λt)^(γ−1) e^(−λt) / Γ(γ). For γ = 2 and λ = 0.0002, determine the expected number of failures in one year of operation.
The failure time of a new brake drum design is observed to follow a Gamma distribution with a p.d.f. of f(t) = λ(λt)^(γ−1) e^(−λt) / Γ(γ). For γ = 2 and λ = 0.0002, determine the MTTF.

The failure time of a new brake drum design is observed to follow a Gamma distribution with a p.d.f. of f(t) = λ(λt)^(γ−1) e^(−λt) / Γ(γ). For γ = 2 and λ = 0.0002, determine the reliability at t = 1000 hours.

A system consists of n components in series. Each component is subject to failure, and its reliability is p = 0.98. The system fails if any two consecutive components fail. Determine the reliability of the system when n = 6.

Consider a system with three components in series. The components have constant failure rates λ1, λ2, and λ3. The failure rate of the third component is three times the failure rate of the second component, and the failure rate of the second component is twice that of the first component. It is desired to achieve a system reliability of 0.95 at time t = 100 hours. Determine the failure rate λ1 of the first component (in failures per hour).

Consider a system with three components in series. The components have constant failure rates λ1, λ2, and λ3. The failure rate of the third component is three times the failure rate of the second component, and the failure rate of the second component is twice that of the first component. It is desired to achieve a system reliability of 0.95 at time t = 100 hours. Determine the failure rate λ2 of the second component (in failures per hour).

Consider a system with three components in series. The components have constant failure rates λ1, λ2, and λ3. The failure rate of the third component is three times the failure rate of the second component, and the failure rate of the second component is twice that of the first component. It is desired to achieve a system reliability of 0.95 at time t = 100 hours. Determine the failure rate λ3 of the third component (in failures per hour).

Consider a system with three components in series. The components have constant failure rates λ1, λ2, and λ3. The failure rate of the third component is three times the failure rate of the second component, and the failure rate of the second component is twice that of the first component. It is desired to achieve a system reliability of 0.95 at time t = 100 hours. Determine the MTTF of the system in hours.

The Special Erlang distribution is useful in modeling the failure rate of many electronic components. The p.d.f. of the distribution is f(t) = (t/λ²) exp(−t/λ), t ≥ 0. Assume that at t = 1000 hours, the hazard rate of a component is 5 × 10^(−4) failures/h. What is the reliability of a system composed of three similar components connected in parallel at time t = 10^4?

The Special Erlang distribution is useful in modeling the failure rate of many electronic components. The p.d.f. of the distribution is f(t) = (t/λ²) exp(−t/λ), t ≥ 0. Assume that at t = 1000 hours, the hazard rate of a component is 5 × 10^(−4) failures/h. What is the MTTF of a system composed of three similar components connected in parallel? Give your answer in hours.

In a 3-out-of-n system with components having a linearly increasing hazard rate h(t) = 0.5 × 10^(−8) t failures/h, determine the number of components for the system such that a reliability of 0.98 is achieved at t = 10^3 hours.

In a 3-out-of-n system with components having a linearly increasing hazard rate h(t) = 0.5 × 10^(−8) t failures/h, the number of components is n = 3 such that a reliability of 0.98 is achieved at t = 10^3 hours. What is the MTTF? Give your answer in hours.

Partial Redundancy is defined as the configuration where at least k out of m (k < m) possible paths must be successful. Consider a system of m diodes (a diode is an electronic device which allows current to pass in one direction and prevents it from passing in the other direction) connected in parallel and each diode is subject to either failures: (i) open, the current cannot pass in through the diode and (ii) short, the diode allows current to pass either way in the circuit. Assume that the probability of a diode failing in the open failure mode is qo, the probability of failing in the short mode is qs, and the probability of working properly is p. Note that qo + qs + p = 1. Assuming that the system is successful if at least k of the m diodes are successful, if m = 6, k = 3, qo = 0.02, and qs = 0.05, what is the reliability of the system?

Given the following failure time data: 40, 45, 55, 68, 78, 85, 94, 99, 120, 140, 160, and 175 hours. Assuming that the data follow an exponential distribution, use the methods of moments to estimate the parameter of the exponential distribution.

Given the following failure time data: 40, 45, 55, 68, 78, 85, 94, 99, 120, 140, 160, and 175 hours. Assuming that the data follow an exponential distribution, what is the reliability of a component that belongs to the same population of tested units at time t = 49 hours?

The failure data in Table 5.20 are obtained from a fatigue test of helical gears (the time between failures are in hours): 220, 400, 590, 790, 1200, 1900, 3000, 4900, 6500, 9000, 14000, 19000, 22000, 25000, 28000, 31000, 35000, 40500, 45000, 49000, 53000, 58000, 64000, 68000, 75000, 100000. If the data fit an exponential distribution, what is the estimated MTBF?

Power supplies are major units for most electronic products. The manufacturers usually use a reliability demonstration test to establish a measure of reliability. For example, to demonstrate a 20 000 hours MTTF, 13 power supplies must be operated at full load for 60 days without observing any failure. To extend the demonstrated MTTF to 200 000 hours, 127 units must be operated over the same duration (Eimar, 1990). A manufacturer subjects 10 power supplies to a reliability test and observes the following times to failure. 10 000, 18 000, 21 000, 22 000, 22 500, 23 000, 25 000, 30 000, 40 000, 70 000 hours. Estimate the parameters of the distribution using three different methods. Give the estimated failure rate λ.

Power supplies are major units for most electronic products. A manufacturer subjects 10 power supplies to a reliability test and observes the following times to failure. 10 000, 18 000, 21 000, 22 000, 22 500, 23 000, 25 000, 30 000, 40 000, 70 000 hours. If you were to own a power supply produced by this manufacturer, what would the reliability of your unit be at t = 20 000 hours?

Power supplies are major units for most electronic products. A manufacturer subjects 10 power supplies to a reliability test and observes the following times to failure. 10 000, 18 000, 21 000, 22 000, 22 500, 23 000, 25 000, 30 000, 40 000, 70 000 hours. Assuming the failure data follow an exponential distribution, what is the estimated MTTF in hours?
Assume f(t) = 1/4, 0 ≤ t ≤ 4 (uniform distribution). Determine the expected number of failures if a replacement occurs every two weeks.

Capacitors are used in electrical circuits whenever radio interference needs to be suppressed. Most radio frequency interference (RFI) capacitors are made from either a metalized plastic film or metalized paper. Metalized paper capacitors often fail after short circuits. But the major advantage of metalized paper over metalized plastic capacitors is their superior self-healing capability under dry conditions. A manufacturer develops capacitors with different structures that result in better performance than both the metalized paper and the metalized plastic capacitors. The manufacturer subjects a capacitor to a transient voltage of 1.2 kV and 10 pulses/day. The duration of each pulse is 10 μs. When a capacitor fails, it is repaired and immediately placed under the same test conditions. Assume that the failure time of the capacitor follows an Erlang distribution with a p.d.f. of f(t) = (t/λ²) e^(−t/λ), where λ is 2 × 10^3 hours. The repair time is exponentially distributed with a repair rate of 4 × 10^2 repairs/h. Determine the expected number of failures during one year of testing (0, 10^4 hours].

Capacitors are used in electrical circuits whenever radio interference needs to be suppressed. Most radio frequency interference (RFI) capacitors are made from either a metalized plastic film or metalized paper. Metalized paper capacitors often fail after short circuits. But the major advantage of metalized paper over metalized plastic capacitors is their superior self-healing capability under dry conditions. A manufacturer develops capacitors with different structures that result in better performance than both the metalized paper and the metalized plastic capacitors. The manufacturer subjects a capacitor to a transient voltage of 1.2 kV and 10 pulses/day. The duration of each pulse is 10 μs. When a capacitor fails, it is repaired and immediately placed under the same test conditions. Assume that the failure time of the capacitor follows an Erlang distribution with a p.d.f. of f(t) = (t/λ²) e^(−t/λ), where λ is 2 × 10^3 hours. The repair time is exponentially distributed with a repair rate of 4 × 10^2 repairs/h. Determine the availability of the capacitor at the end of the testing period.

A system composed of 100 identical components and its operation is independent of the number of failed units at any time t. Assume the failures constitute an NHPP with failure-rate function λ(t) given by: λ(t) = 0.001 + 0.0002t for 0 ≤ t ≤ 200 hours; λ(t) = 0.041 for 200 ≤ t ≤ 300 hours; λ(t) = 0.044 − 0.00001t for 300 ≤ t ≤ 400 hours; and λ(t) = 0.007t for t > 400. What is the expected number of failures in the interval (200, 600]?

In a block replacement policy, the cost of a failure replacement is $150 while the cost of a preventive replacement is $80. Assume that the failure times of a component that is replaced, based on the block replacement policy, follow a beta distribution. The parameters of the distribution are α = 4 and β = 3 hours. The p.d.f. of the beta distribution is f(t) = [Γ(α + β) / (Γ(α)Γ(β))] t^(α−1) (1 − t)^(β−1), 0 < t < 1. What is the optimal replacement interval?

In a block replacement policy, the cost of a failure replacement is $150 while the cost of a preventive replacement is $80. Assume that the failure times of a component that is replaced, based on the block replacement policy, follow a beta distribution. The parameters of the distribution are α = 4 and β = 3 hours. The p.d.f. of the beta distribution is f(t) = [Γ(α + β) / (Γ(α)Γ(β))] t^(α−1) (1 − t)^(β−1), 0 < t < 1. Assume that the penalty factor for surplus or shortage inventory (amount of stock that meets the expected failures during the replacement interval) is $20. Assuming that there are 200 units in operation, what is the optimal stock level L?

A manufacturer wishes to estimate the warranty cost for 3000 products. Assume that every time a repair is performed, it decreases the mean time to the next failure. The field data show that the failure-time distribution function is exponential with different means, that is F_i(t) = 1 − e^(−λ_i t) with λ_1 < λ_2 < λ_3 < ⋯ < λ_w, where λ_i is the failure rate of the last failure before the expiration of the warranty length, w. The manufacturer wishes to extend the warranty for three months. The failure rates of the first five failures are 0.5, 0.8, 1, 1.2, and 3 failures/yr. The corresponding repair costs are 20, 19, 18, 18, 18. Determine the total warranty cost.

The failure distribution of a product consists of a mixture of two exponential densities that exhibit the desired failure-rate characteristics. This can be interpreted as having a mixture of two kinds of units: a proportion p of defectives, with a high failure rate λ1, and a proportion 1 − p of "normal" units with a lower failure rate λ2. The p.d.f. of the model is f(t) = pλ1 e^(−λ1 t) + (1 − p)λ2 e^(−λ2 t). Assume λ1 = 4 failures/yr, λ2 = 3 failures/yr, and the cost per repair is $80. Determine the warranty cost for a warranty length of three years. Also, assume p = 0.3.

A system made up of 5 series components has a design reliability of .96. Subsequently, 4 of the components have reliabilities apportioned to them of 0.992, 0.991, 0.994, and 0.992. What should the reliability apportionment for the 5th subsystem be ?

What is the reliability of this system ? This is a system with five components A, B, C, D and E. The system is connected in a bridge system configuration: There are two main paths: A->B and C->D. Component E creates a bridge betwwen the two paths: so A->E->D and C->E->B are also possible. Component reliabilities are A = 0.80        B = 0.95         C = 0.82 D = 0.85        E = 0.75
The failure rate for an IC chip is 0.0023 per hour of operation. Calculate the MTBF given that the failure rate is constant.
Given mean-time-to-failure of 200 hours for each of two components, what is the probability of failure if both components operate in series for one hour ?

The reliability goal for a complex system is 0.995. The predicted reliability for the system is 0.999 when one of the subsystems is functioning and 0.90 when that subsystem is not functioning. What is the minimum reliability for this subsystem to meet the system reliability goal ?
What is System Effectiveness, if Operational Readiness is 0.89, Design Adequacy is 95%, Availability is 98%, Maintainability is 0.93, and Mission Reliability is 0.99 ?
An industrial compressor operated for 8000 hours in continuous service with 5 breakdowns whose respective durations are: 7; 22; 8.5; 3.5 and 9 hours. Determine its MTBF.

An industrial compressor operated for 8000 hours in continuous service with 5 breakdowns whose respective durations are: 7; 22; 8.5; 3.5 and 9 hours. Determine its failure rate λ (in failures per hour).

A production system consists of 4 machines connected in series with failure rates per 1000 hours of: 0.052; 0.059; 0.044 and 0.048 respectively. What is the probability that the system operates without failure up to 4000 hours?
A production system consists of 4 machines connected in series with failure rates per 1000 hours of: 0.052; 0.059; 0.044 and 0.048 respectively. Determine the MTBF of the system (in hours).

A production machine with a total operating time of 1500 hours consists of four subsystems A, B, C and D connected in series with the following respective MTBFs: MTBFA = 4500 hours, MTBFB = 3200 hours, MTBFC = 6000 hours, MTBFD = 10500 hours. Determine the overall failure rate λS (in failures per hour).

A production machine with a total operating time of 1500 hours consists of four subsystems A, B, C and D connected in series with the following respective MTBFs: MTBFA = 4500 hours, MTBFB = 3200 hours, MTBFC = 6000 hours, MTBFD = 10500 hours. Determine the overall MTBF of the system MTBFS (in hours).

A production machine with a total operating time of 1500 hours consists of four subsystems A, B, C and D connected in series with the following respective MTBFs: MTBFA = 4500 hours, MTBFB = 3200 hours, MTBFC = 6000 hours, MTBFD = 10500 hours. Determine the overall reliability RS of the machine for 1500 hours.

A production machine consists of four subsystems A, B, C and D connected in series with the following respective MTBFs: MTBFA = 4500 hours, MTBFB = 3200 hours, MTBFC = 6000 hours, MTBFD = 10500 hours. If the operating time of the machine is halved to 750 hours, determine the overall reliability RS.

A production machine consists of four subsystems A, B, C and D connected in series with the following respective MTBFs: MTBFA = 4500 hours, MTBFB = 3200 hours, MTBFC = 6000 hours, MTBFD = 10500 hours. What is the probability that the system operates without failure up to 5000 hours?
Consider a printer consisting of 2000 components connected in series, all assumed to have the same very high reliability R = 0.9999. Determine the reliability of the device.

Consider a printer consisting of components connected in series, all assumed to have the same very high reliability R = 0.9999. If the number of components is halved to 1000, determine the reliability of the device.

Consider a printer consisting of 2000 components connected in series. If a reliability of 90% is desired for the entire set of 2000 components connected in series, determine the reliability that each component must have.
Three devices A, B and C with the same reliability RA = RB = RC = 0.75 are connected in parallel. Determine the reliability of the system.
Two devices with the same reliability R = 0.75 are connected in parallel. Determine the reliability of the system.
Four devices with the same reliability R = 0.75 are connected in parallel. Determine the reliability of the system.
Devices with the same reliability R = 0.75 are connected in parallel. How many devices in parallel are needed to achieve an overall reliability of 0.999 (99.9%)?

If an overall reliability of 99% is desired with only three devices connected in parallel, what should be the reliability R of each of these devices? The system reliability is given by RS = 1 − (1 − R)^3.
